title: Uncinate joint stabilizers and associated systems and methods
abstract: A method for stabilizing a cervical spine segment includes implanting a respective uncinate joint stabilizer into each uncinate joint of the cervical spine segment to stabilize the uncinate joints and thereby stabilize the cervical spine segment. A system for stabilizing a cervical spine segment includes a pair of uncinate joint stabilizers for stabilizing a respective pair of uncinate joints of the cervical spine segment, wherein each uncinate joint stabilizer is elongated along a lengthwise dimension and configured for placement in the respective uncinate joint with the lengthwise dimension substantially oriented along an anterior-to-posterior direction of the cervical spine segment, and wherein each uncinate joint stabilizer has height in a heightwise dimension orthogonal to the lengthwise dimension and the height is configured to define spacing of the respective uncinate joint.
claims:
Claims CLAIMS What is claimed is: 1. A method for stabilizing a cervical spine segment, comprising: implanting a respective uncinate joint stabilizer into each uncinate joint of the cervical spine segment to stabilize the uncinate joints and thereby stabilize the cervical spine segment. 2. The method of claim 1, in the step of implanting, each uncinate joint stabilizer being configured to preserve motion of the respective uncinate joint. 3. The method of claim 1, in the step of implanting, each uncinate joint stabilizer being configured to promote fusion of the respective uncinate joint. 4. The method of claim 10, in the step of implanting, each uncinate joint stabilizer being a temporary uncinate joint stabilizer composed of a biodegradable material, for temporary stabilization of the respective uncinate joint. 5. The method of claim 1, in the step of implanting, each uncinate joint stabilizer being composed of a non-fusing material. 6. The method of claim 1, in the step of implanting, the uncinate joint stabilizers being configured to cooperate to bear at least a portion of load of the cervical spine segment to maintain spacing between cervical vertebrae of the cervical spine segment. 7. The method of claim 1, the step of implanting comprising inserting each uncinate joint stabilizer along an anterior-to-posterior direction. 8. The method of claim 7, the step of inserting comprising inserting each uncinate joint stabilizer into the respective uncinate joint while leaving intervertebral disc of the cervical spine segment undisturbed. 9. The method of claim 7, the step of inserting comprising inserting each uncinate joint stabilizer percutaneously. 10. The method of claim 9, each uncinate joint stabilizer being cannulated, the step of inserting comprising inserting each uncinate joint stabilizer over a guide wire. 11. The method of claim 10, the step of inserting the guide wire comprising inserting the guide wire under imaging guidance. 12. The method of claim 9, the step of inserting comprising inserting each uncinate joint stabilizer through a respective cannula. 13. The method of claim 7, further comprising, during the step of inserting each uncinate joint stabilizer: monitoring, using imaging guidance, locations of (a) each uncinate joint stabilizer and (b) instrumentation used to insert each uncinate joint stabilizer. 14. The method of claim 13, the step of monitoring comprising monitoring the locations relative to vertebral artery on side of spine associated with the uncinate joint stabilizer, to prevent damaging the vertebral artery. 15. The method of claim 7, further comprising: distracting each uncinate joint. 16. The method of claim 15, the step of distracting comprising: distracting each uncinate joint using a distraction tool; and removing the distraction tool from the uncinate joint prior to the step of implanting. 17. The method of claim 15, the step of distracting comprising distracting each uncinate joint using the respective uncinate joint stabilizer during the step of inserting. 18. The method of claim 7, each uncinate joint stabilizer having threads, the step of inserting comprising threading each uncinate joint stabilizer into the respective uncinate joint using the threads. 19. The method of claim 18, further comprising: in the step of threading, threading each uncinate joint stabilizer into the respective uncinate joint such that the threads contact uncinate joint surfaces only of a selected one of superior and inferior vertebral bodies of the cervical spine segment; and securing each uncinate joint stabilizer to only the selected one of superior and inferior vertebral bodies to preserve motion of the cervical spine segment. 20. The method of claim 19, the step of securing comprising utilizing a tension band formed by at least one ligament of the cervical spine segment. 21. The method of claim 19, the step of securing comprising, for each uncinate joint stabilizer, affixing mounting hardware to the uncinate joint stabilizer and the vertebral body. 22. The method of claim 18, in the step of inserting, and for each uncinate joint, the threads contacting both superior and inferior surfaces of the uncinate joint. 23. The method of claim 7, in the step of implanting, each uncinate joint stabilizer including bone graft material to promote fusion of the respective uncinate joint. 24. The method of claim 1, further comprising: in the step of implanting, inserting each uncinate joint stabilizer into the respective uncinate joint along a medial -to-lateral direction starting from intervertebral disc space of the cervical spine segment; and securing each uncinate joint stabilizer to the respective uncinate joint. 25. The method of claim 24, in the step of inserting each uncinate joint stabilizer, each uncinate joint stabilizer including a tapered implant element for interfacing with superior and inferior uncinate joint surfaces, the step of inserting each uncinate joint stabilizer comprising for each uncinate joint: distracting the uncinate joint by inserting the uncinate joint stabilizer into the uncinate joint along the medial -to-lateral direction with thinner portion of the tapered implant element facing the uncinate joint. 26. The method of claim 25, the step of securing comprising: securing each uncinate joint stabilizer to the respective uncinate joint using features protruding from the tapered implant element. 27. The method of claim 25, the step of securing comprising: securing each uncinate joint stabilizer to the respective uncinate joint by, in part, utilizing tension band formed by at least one ligament associated with the respective uncinate joint. 28. The method of claim 25, further comprising before the step of distracting: opening anterior portion of the intervertebral disc space to open access path for the uncinate joint stabilizers. 29. The method of claim 25, further comprising: in the step of distracting, temporarily securing each uncinate joint stabilizer to the respective uncinate joint using features protruding from the tapered implant element; after the step of distracting, performing cervical discectomy to remove at least a portion of intervertebral disc; after the step of performing cervical discectomy, further distracting each uncinate joint by moving, along the medial -to-lateral direction, each uncinate joint stabilizer further into the respective uncinate joint; and after the step of further distracting, securing each uncinate joint stabilizer to the respective uncinate joint using the features protruding from the tapered implant element. 30. The method of claim 29, each of the steps of distracting, performing cervical discectomy, and further distracting comprising moving each uncinate joint stabilizer using an actuator mechanically coupled with the uncinate joint stabilizers. 31. The method of claim 24, further comprising, before the step of inserting each uncinate joint stabilizer: opening anterior portion of the intervertebral disc space to open access path for a pair of tapered trial implants; and distracting the uncinate joints by inserting each tapered trial implant into a respective uncinate joint along the medial -to-lateral direction. 32. The method of claim 31, the step of distracting comprising using an actuator to insert each tapered trial implant into the respective uncinate joint to distract the respective uncinate joint, the actuator accessing the tapered trial implants from anterior side of the cervical spine segment. 33. The method of claim 32, further comprising: temporarily securing each tapered trial implant to the respective uncinate joint using features protruding from the tapered trial implant. 34. The method of claim 32, the step of distracting comprising using the actuator to distract both uncinate joints simultaneously. 35. The method of claim 31, further comprising, after the step of distracting and before the step of inserting each uncinate joint stabilizer: performing cervical discectomy to remove at least a portion of intervertebral disc of the cervical spine segment. 36. The method of claim 35, the step of performing cervical discectomy comprising: mechanically coupling discectomy instrumentation to the tapered trial implants; and using the discectomy instrumentation to remove the at least a portion of intervertebral disc. 37. The method of claim 36, the discectomy instrumentation including a soft tissue retractor. 38. The method of claim 35, further comprising removing each tapered trial implant after the step of performing cervical discectomy; and the step of inserting each uncinate joint stabilizer further comprising distracting each uncinate joint by moving, along a medial -to-lateral direction, each uncinate joint stabilizer into the respective uncinate joint. 39. The method of claim 31, the step of inserting each uncinate joint stabilizer comprising moving the uncinate joint stabilizer using an actuator mechanically coupled with the uncinate joint stabilizers. 40. The method of claim 24, further comprising, after the step of inserting each uncinate joint stabilizer: depositing bone graft material in the intervertebral disc space to promote fusion of the cervical spine segment. 41. The method of claim 24, further comprising, after the step of inserting each uncinate joint stabilizer: placing an intervertebral-disc-space (IVDS) implant in the intervertebral disc space. 42. The method of claim 41, the step of placing an IVDS implant comprising mechanically coupling the IVDS implant with the uncinate joint stabilizers. 43. The method of claim 42, the step of mechanically coupling comprising adjusting lateral dimension of the IVDS implant to match distance between the uncinate joint stabilizers. 44. The method of claim 42, the step of securing each uncinate joint stabilizer comprising utilizing mechanical coupling with the IVDS implant. 45. A system for stabilizing a cervical spine segment, comprising: a pair of uncinate joint stabilizers for stabilizing a respective pair of uncinate joints of the cervical spine segment, each uncinate joint stabilizer being elongated along a lengthwise dimension and configured for placement in the respective uncinate joint with the lengthwise dimension substantially oriented along an anterior-to-posterior direction of the cervical spine segment, each uncinate joint stabilizer having height in a heightwise dimension orthogonal to the lengthwise dimension, the height being configured to define spacing of the respective uncinate joint. 46. The system of claim 45, the height being in range from two millimeters to seven millimeters. 47. The system of claim 45, the length being in range from six to eighteen millimeters. 48. The system of claim 45, each uncinate joint stabilizer having length in the lengthwise dimension and width in a widthwise dimension orthogonal to the lengthwise and heightwise dimensions, the width being less than the length and greater than the height. 49. The system of claim 48, the width and height being compatible with percutaneous insertion of each uncinate joint stabilizer into the respective uncinate joint along an anterior-to-posterior direction. 50. The system of claim 48, each of the width and the height being no greater than 10 millimeters. 51. The system of claim 45, each uncinate joint stabilizer comprising a textured surface for securing the uncinate joint stabilizer to the respective uncinate joint. 52. The system of claim 45, each uncinate joint stabilizer being configured for insertion into the respective uncinate joint along an anterior to posterior direction, the system further comprising, for each uncinate joint stabilizer: a locking lever coupled to trailing or leading end of the uncinate joint stabilizer, interface between the uncinate joint stabilizer and the locking lever being configured to allow rotation of the locking lever about an axis in the lengthwise dimension, extent of the locking lever in a first dimension transverse to the lengthwise dimension being less than the height to allow insertion of the uncinate joint stabilizer without the locking lever contacting surfaces of the respective uncinate joint, extent of the locking lever in a second dimension orthogonal to the first dimension and the lengthwise dimension being greater than the height to lock the locking lever to at least one surface of the uncinate joint by rotating the locking lever about the axis so as to secure the uncinate joint stabilizer in the respective uncinate joint. 53. The system of claim 52, the locking lever including a jagged surface for locking the locking lever to the at least one surface. 54. The system of claim 52, when the locking lever is oriented to align the second dimension with the heightwise dimension, the locking lever extending beyond the uncinate joint stabilizer in both directions away from the axis along the heightwise dimension, to enable locking of the locking lever to both superior and inferior surfaces of the respective uncinate joint. 55. The system of claim 52, when the locking lever is oriented to align the second dimension with the heightwise dimension, the locking lever extending beyond the uncinate joint stabilizer only in one direction away from the axis along the heightwise dimension, to enable locking of the locking lever to only one of superior and inferior surfaces of the respective uncinate joint. 56. The system of claim 45, further comprising, for each uncinate joint stabilizer, a screw coupled to the uncinate joint stabilizer for threading the uncinate joint stabilizer into the respective uncinate joint along the anterior-to-posterior direction by contacting threads of the screw to at least one surface of the respective uncinate joint. 57. The system of claim 56, each uncinate joint stabilizer having two openings that allow the threads to contact the superior and inferior surfaces, respectively, for inserting the uncinate joint stabilizer into the respective uncinate joint by threading the screw into the respective uncinate joint, interface between the uncinate joint stabilizer and the screw allowing for rotation of the uncinate joint stabilizer about axis of screw to rotate the uncinate joint stabilizer around the screw to secure the uncinate joint stabilizer to superior and inferior surfaces of the uncinate joint and define the spacing after inserting the uncinate joint stabilizer into the respective uncinate joint. 58. The system of claim 57, each uncinate joint stabilizer having jagged exterior surfaces for securing the uncinate joint stabilizer to the superior and inferior surfaces. 59. The system of claim 45, each uncinate joint stabilizer comprising a generally cylindrical portion with cylinder axis in the lengthwise dimension, the generally cylindrical portion having threads for threading the uncinate joint stabilizer into the respective uncinate joint along the anterior-to-posterior direction. 60. The system of claim 59, the generally cylindrical portion having diameter to implement the height. 61. The system of claim 59, each uncinate joint stabilizer being configured to expose the threads at least in two opposite-facing directions in the heightwise dimension, to secure the uncinate joint stabilizer to both superior and inferior surfaces of the respective uncinate joint using the threads. 62. The system of claim 61, the generally cylindrical portion comprising a porous portion for accommodating bone graft material and bone growth. 63. The system of claim 59, the threads being interrupted by one or more fenestrations, oriented along the lengthwise direction, the fenestrations being configured to accommodate one or more materials selected from the group consisting of bone graft 

 material, bone growth, and tissue displaced from the respective uncinate joint by the uncinate joint stabilizer. 64. The system of claim 59, each uncinate joint stabilizer being cannulated along the cylinder axis for insertion into the respective uncinate joint over a respective guide wire. 65. The system of claim 64, further comprising an interface for coupling the uncinate joint stabilizer to a drill for drilling the uncinate joint stabilizer into the respective uncinate joint over the respective guide wire. 66. The system of claim 65, the interface being located on an end face of the uncinate joint stabilizer, the end face generally facing along the lengthwise direction. 67. The system of claim 59, each uncinate joint stabilizer comprising a tapered portion that (a) is adjacent to the generally cylindrical portion, (b) is offset from the generally cylindrical portion in a direction along the lengthwise dimension, and (c) has decreasing extent transverse to the cylinder axis with increasing distance along the cylinder axis away from the generally cylindrical portion, such that the tapered portion eases insertion of the uncinate joint stabilizer into the respective uncinate joint along the anterior-to-posterior direction. 68. The system of claim 59, each uncinate joint stabilizer further comprising a housing for partly containing the generally cylindrical portion, the housing including: an opening from which the threads protrude in a first direction along the heightwise dimension, to allow the threads to contact a selected one of superior and inferior surfaces of the respective uncinate joint; and a material portion shielding the threads from contacting a non-selected one of the superior and inferior surfaces. 69. The system of claim 68, the material portion comprising a smooth surface for contacting the non-selected one of the superior and inferior surfaces while allowing relative movement between the uncinate joint stabilizer and the non-selected one of the superior and inferior surfaces, to form a motion-preserving uncinate joint stabilizer. 70. The system of claim 68, further comprising mounting hardware for affixing each uncinate joint stabilizer to vertebra associated with the selected one of the superior and inferior surfaces. 71. The system of claim 45, each uncinate joint stabilizer including a tapered portion for interfacing with superior and inferior surfaces of the respective uncinate joint, the tapered portion having a gradient in height along the widthwise dimension, to enable insertion of the uncinate joint stabilizer into the respective uncinate joint from intervertebral disc space of the cervical spine segment. 72. The system of claim 71, the tapered portion having (a) a first surface for contacting the superior surface of the respective uncinate joint and (b) a second surface for contacting the inferior surface of the respective uncinate joint, each of the first and second surfaces having at least one protruding feature for securing the uncinate joint stabilizer to the respective uncinate joint. 73. The system of claim 71, the tapered portion having taper angle in range between 10Â° and 45Â°. 74. The system of claim 45, each uncinate joint stabilizer being composed of metal. 75. The system of claim 74, the metal being selected from the group consisting of titanium, titanium alloy, stainless steel, cobalt, chromium, and a combination thereof. 76. The system of claim 45, each uncinate joint stabilizer including a polymer. 77. The system of claim 76, the polymer being selected from the group consisting of (a) polyetheretherketone (PEEK) and (b) other polyaryletherketone (PAEK) material. 78. The system of claim 45 being composed of biodegradable material. 79. The system of claim 45 being composed of a non-fusing material. 80. The system of claim 78, the biodegradable material being selected from the group consisting of lactulose, proline, polyglycolic acid, a derivative of polyglycolic acid, poly-L-lactic acid, a derivative of poly-L-lactic acid, and a combination thereof. 81. A system for distracting uncinate joints of a cervical spine segment, comprising: two tapered elements; and an actuator configured to couple with the tapered elements and change distance between the tapered elements, to insert the tapered elements into the uncinate joints, respectively, from intervertebral disc space of the cervical spine segment. 82. The system of claim 81, each of the tapered elements being elongated in a lengthwise dimension and having height in a heightwise dimension orthogonal to the lengthwise dimension, the height having a gradient in a widthwise dimension orthogonal to the lengthwise and heightwise dimensions to define tapering of the tapered element. 83. The system of claim 82, further comprising two extensions for extending the tapered elements, respectively, along the lengthwise dimension, each extension having length sufficient to protrude in anterior direction from the respective uncinate joint, the actuator being coupled to the tapered elements via the extensions. 84. The system of claim 83, each extension including an interface for holding instrumentation for protecting soft tissue. 85. The system of claim 83, each extension being integrally formed with the respective tapered element. 86. The system of claim 83, each extension being removably coupled with the respective tapered element. 87. The system of claim 81, each tapered element including polymer. 88. The system of claim 87, each tapered element including at least one portion for contacting surfaces of the respective uncinate joint, the portion being composed of polymer. 89. The system of claim 81, the tapered elements tapering away from each other when coupled with the actuator, such that the height of each implant decreases in direction away from the other implant. 90. The system of claim 89, each tapered element tapering from height less than one millimeter to height no greater than six millimeters. 91. The system of claim 89, each tapered element tapering from height less than two millimeters to height greater than two millimeters. 92. The system of claim 89, each tapered element including features protruding from the tapered element for temporarily securing the tapered elements to the uncinate joints. 93. The system of claim 81, further including a coupling mechanism for coupling and uncoupling the actuator with the tapered elements. 94. The system of claim 81, the actuator including: two connecting members for mechanically coupling with the tapered elements, respectively; two handles for actuating the actuator; and a hinge, coupled with the connecting members and the handles, for translating action of the handles to the connecting members. 95. The system of claim 94, the hinge being configured for reverse scissor action. 96. The system of claim 94, the hinge being configured for scissor action. 97. The system of claim 81, further comprising an indicator for indicating separation between the tapered elements when coupled with the actuator.
